{"key_event":"Qajar decentralization and foreign concessionary pressures","reasoning":"Under Naser al-Din Shah, the Qajar state had a central court, governors, taxation claims, and coercive forces, but its reach was limited by tribes, provincial notables, clerics, foreign influence, and weak fiscal-administrative capacity. Civil society was not atomized—ulama networks, bazaars, guilds, tribes, and local communities had real autonomous influence—but nationwide organized accountability was still sporadic before the Tobacco Protest and Constitutional Revolution.","society_change":0.0,"society_power":4.0,"state_change":0.0,"state_power":3.5}
